title: Setting Up an IV Infusion
oneLiner: Process of delivering intravenous fluids or medications directly into the bloodstream for resuscitation, maintenance, or drug administration – a core resuscitation and ward skill.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
A 72-year-old man is admitted from a care home with two days of diarrhoea and vomiting. He is drowsy, has sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, slow cap refill (~4 seconds), and a BP of 85/50 mmHg with a heart rate of 110 bpm. His urine output over the last 4 hours is negligible. Your FY1 colleague calls you to "set up an IV infusion" for him.

- **Drowsy, dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes** → severe dehydration (fluid loss >10% body weight)
- **Slow cap refill, tachycardia, hypotension** → hypovolaemic shock – immediate need for rapid volume expansion
- **Low urine output (<0.5 ml/kg/hr)** → hypoperfusion of kidneys; fluid resuscitation may prevent acute kidney injury
- **Diarrhoea and vomiting** → isotonic fluid loss (Na+ ~140 mmol/L lost), so mainstay is crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann's / 0.9% saline)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- The "silent hypovolaemia": elderly patient with isolated confusion, no tachycardia (beta-blocker) → check lying/standing BP, cap refill
- The "wet hypovolaemia": patient presents with oedema (e.g. liver disease) but is intravascularly depleted – need careful fluid challenge
- The "distributive" mimic: septic patient with vasodilation looks similar but needs different fluid strategy (balanced crystalloid + early antibiotics)
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma: IV infusion is the delivery of a sterile fluid (crystalloid or colloid) into a vein via a cannula, giving set, and infusion pump or drip stand. It serves three main clinical purposes: **resuscitation** (rapid bolus to correct hypovolaemia), **maintenance** (replace daily losses when oral is impossible), and **replacement** (match ongoing losses like diarrhoea or NG output). It matters because it is the most common intervention in hospitalised patients, directly impacts haemodynamics, electrolyte balance, and outcomes. Common pitfalls: giving the wrong fluid (e.g. 0.9% saline in hyperchloraemic acidosis), too fast (fluid overload), too slow (inadequate resuscitation), or failing to monitor.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Fluid compartments:** Total body water ~60% in men, ~50% in women. 2/3 is intracellular (ICF), 1/3 extracellular (ECF). ECF divides into interstitial (75%) and intravascular (25%). Losses from ECF (e.g. diarrhoea) shrink both interstitial and intravascular spaces – hence dry mucous membranes (interstitial) and low BP (intravascular).

**Starling forces:** Capillary hydrostatic pressure pushes fluid out; plasma oncotic pressure (mainly albumin) pulls fluid in. In hypovolaemia, hydrostatic pressure falls → reduced filtration → tissue dehydration. IV crystalloids distribute quickly across the ECF (only ~25% remains intravascular after 1 hour). Colloids (e.g. Gelofusine) stay longer intravascularly but carry risks (renal injury, cost).

**Why signs occur:**
- **Tachycardia** → baroreceptor reflex activated by ↓BP, sympathetic output increases HR to maintain cardiac output
- **Hypotension** → decreased stroke volume (↓preload) → reduced cardiac output → low BP
- **Slow cap refill** → vasoconstriction in skin (compensatory to preserve vital organ perfusion)
- **Oliguria** → renal hypoperfusion triggers renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system – conserves water and Na+

**Connecting to pattern recognition:** The vignette's vital signs signal a severe ECF deficit. Rapid infusion of a balanced crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann's) will acutely expand the intravascular compartment, lifting BP, heart rate improves, and urine output resumes – confirming the diagnosis.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
Indications for IV infusion fall into clinical scenarios – think of as "why does this patient need a drip?":

| Category | Common Cause | One-line "stem clue" |
|----------|--------------|----------------------|
| **Hypovolaemic** | Haemorrhage (trauma, GI bleed) | "Bright red per rectum" or "open fracture" |
| | Dehydration (vomiting/diarrhoea) | "Sunken eyes, reduced skin turgor" |
| | Burns (major) | "Blistering over 20% BSA" |
| **Distributive** | Sepsis | "Hot, tachycardic, hypotensive, history of infection" |
| | Anaphylaxis | "Sudden onset urticaria + stridor after drug" |
| | Pancreatitis | "Epigastric pain radiating to back, raised amylase" |
| **Maintenance** | Nil by mouth (pre-op, ileus) | "NBM since midnight" |
| | Impaired swallowing (CVA, frailty) | "Drooling, poor gag" |
| **Replacement** | High stoma output | "Dark green liquid in bag >1.5 L/day" |
| | NG losses | "2000 ml aspirate per shift" |

**Most common cause in general medical/surgical patients:** Dehydration from oral route failure (infection, post-op ileus, gastroenteritis).
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
You don't need a formal "diagnosis" to start IV fluids – it's clinical. But investigate to guide **type** and **monitoring**.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why order it | How to interpret |
|---------------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| **Urea & Electrolytes** | Urea, creatinine, Na, K, Cl, HCO3 | Assess renal function, electrolyte derangements, and type of dehydration (isotonic/hypertonic/hypotonic) | Raised urea:creatinine ratio (>20:1) suggests prerenal AKI (needs fluids). Low Na? Use isotonic fluids. High K? Avoid potassium-containing fluids until corrected. |
| **Full Blood Count** | Hb, HCT, WCC | Rule out haemorrhage (falling Hb), infection (raised WCC), or haemoconcentration | HCT >0.45 in a male with dehydration suggests hypovolaemia. Falling Hb + tachycardia = consider bleeding. |
| **Lactate** | Venous/arterial lactate | Marker of tissue hypoperfusion | Lactate >2 mmol/L suggests unmet oxygen demand – fluid resuscitation indicated. Normalising lactate = good. |
| **Urine output** | ml/kg/hr | Most sensitive monitor of end-organ perfusion | <0.5 ml/kg/hr for >2 hours → likely hypoperfusion. |
| **Blood gas (VBG/ABG)** | pH, pCO2, HCO3, lactate, base excess | Identify metabolic acidosis (e.g. lactate, HCO3 dropped) | Base deficit <-5 mmol/L = need for volume expansion. |
| **CVP** (central line) | Central venous pressure | Used in ICU to guide fluid responsiveness (not routine on ward) | CVP <8 mmHg suggests hypovolaemia – but dynamic measures (passive leg raise) are better. |

**Single finding that confirms urgent need for IV infusion:** A lactate >4 mmol/L with hypotension – start 500 ml bolus immediately, while ordering cultures and bloods.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
#### Immediate (first hour) — Resuscitation focus
- **Identify need:** Is this shock? (SBP <90, HR >100, lactate >2, altered mental state)
- **Gain access:** Large-bore peripheral cannula (18G or bigger) in antecubital fossa or forearm
- **Start with:** 500 ml bolus of **Hartmann's solution** (sodium 131, chloride 111, lactate 29 mmol/L) over 10-15 minutes (or via pressure bag if critical)
- **Why Hartmann's?** It is isotonic, balanced – less hyperchloraemic acidosis risk than 0.9% saline. For haemorrhage, give blood first (O negative) if available.
- **Reassess immediately:** After 500 ml, repeat pulse, BP, cap refill, lactate, urine output
- **If no improvement:** Give another 500 ml. If still hypotensive → call senior, consider other causes (sepsis, bleeding) and need for vasopressors / blood

#### Ward (24-72h) — Maintenance and replacement
Once shock corrected, move to maintenance or replacement:

- **Maintenance:** Use **Plasmalyte 148** or **Hartmann's** at 1 ml/kg/hr (e.g., 75-year-old 70 kg → 70 ml/hr). Monitor U&E daily.
- **Replacement:** Add on losses – e.g. diarrhoea 1 L/day → replace ml-for-ml with Hartmann's.
- **Potassium:** If K+ low, add KCl to the bag (e.g., 20 mmol KCl over 4 hours, peripheral line – concentration ≤40 mmol/L to avoid phlebitis).
- **Monitor:** Urine output >0.5 ml/kg/hr, daily weight (best indicator of fluid status), check for oedema, crackles on chest auscultation.
- **Stop early:** As soon as patient can eat and drink adequately – early feeding reduces infection risk.

#### Long-term — Tailored to underlying condition
- **Home IV therapy** (e.g., short bowel, stoma losses): Consider peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC) for long-term access. Use customised bags (macronutrients ± electrolytes). Monitor for line infections, thrombosis.
- **Renal impairment:** In established AKI, restrict fluids to insensible losses + urine output. Use hypertonic solutions with caution (risk of hypernatraemia).
- **Heart failure:** Fluid overload risk – give cautiously, consider furosemide if signs of failure.

#### Common pitfalls
- **DO NOT** give Hartmann's in the same line as blood (calcium chelates citrate → clot) – flush or use separate access.
- **DO NOT** use 0.9% saline for large volume resuscitation (>2 L) – causes hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis and may worsen AKI.
- **DO NOT** forget to reassess after each 500 ml bolus – fluid overload is as dangerous as under-resuscitation.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
IV infusion is a treatment, not a disease. But the clinical scenario of "patient needing IV fluids" can be mimicked by conditions that look like hypovolaemia but are not:

| Feature | Hypovolaemic Shock (need IV fluids) | Cardiogenic Shock (need inotropes/PCI) | Septic Shock (fluids + antibiotics) | Anaphylaxis (fluids + adrenaline) |
|---------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **History** | Vomiting, diarrhoea, blood loss | Chest pain, prior MI, dyspnoea | Fever, UTI, pneumonia | Drug/insect exposure, sudden onset |
| **Exam** | Dry mucous membranes, reduced turgor, cool peripheries+ | Raised JVP, lung crackles, cold/clammy | Warm peripheries initially, bounding pulse, fever | Urticaria, wheeze, stridor, swelling |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia | ST elevation / new LBBB / ischaemia | Sinus tachycardia ± non-specific changes | Sinus tachycardia |
| **Lactate** | Elevated (tissue hypoperfusion) | Elevated (hypoperfusion) | Elevated (aerobic glycolysis) | May be normal early |
| **Blood pressure** | Low, narrow pulse pressure | Low, often with raised diastolic | Low despite wide pulse pressure | Low (distributive) |
| **Response to 500 ml bolus** | BP improves, HR ↓, urine output up | No improvement or worsens (fluid overload) | May improve, but often needs vasopressors | Improves transiently; needs adrenaline |
| **Key differentiator** | Fluid losses | Raised JVP + crackles | Fever + history | Itch + stridor + exposure |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
Miss the need for IV fluids → irreversible shock → acute kidney injury (AKI), ischemic hepatitis, lactic acidosis, multi-organ failure, death. Conversely, giving fluid when not needed (e.g., cardiogenic shock) → pulmonary oedema, hypoxia, intubation.

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Fluid overload** | Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, leg oedema | Crackles on auscultation, raised JVP | Stop fluid, give furosemide 40 mg IV (if hypotensive – discuss with senior first) |
| **Phlebitis** | Pain, redness, swelling along vein | Local tenderness, palpable cord | Remove cannula, rest, warm compress, consider anticoagulation if extensive |
| **Infection** (septic thrombophlebitis) | Fever, rigors, swollen limb | Purulent discharge from cannula site, + blood cultures | Remove cannula, culture tip, start empirical antibiotics (flucloxacillin + gentamicin if severe) |
| **Air embolism** | Sudden chest pain, dyspnoea, confusion | Tachycardia, hypotension, "millwheel" murmur (rare) | Left lateral Trendelenburg position, 100% O2, call crash team |
| **Electrolyte derangement** | Weakness, arrhythmia, confusion (e.g. hyponatraemia, hypo-/hyperkalaemia) | Check daily U&E | Adjust fluid composition (e.g., add KCl if low, avoid 0.9% saline if hypernatraemic) |
| **Extravasation** (especially with irritant drugs) | Pain, swelling, blistering at site | Device not flushed well, palpable swelling around vein | Stop infusion, aspirate drug, elevate limb, apply cold/warm compresses, refer to tissue viability team |

**Late-presentation patterns (stems that test "what happens if untreated"):**
- Post-operative patient with low urine output and confusion: missed hypovolaemia → AKI → need for dialysis
- Elderly patient with pneumonia: given 2 L saline, develops pulmonary oedema → intubation
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **"S.A.L.T."** for choosing crystalloid type:  
  - **S**odium content (isotonic with plasma)  
  - **A**cid-base balance (avoid hyperchloraemia)  
  - **L**actate / buffer (Hartmann's has lactate, metabolised to bicarb)  
  - **T**onicity (only isotonic for peripheral infusion)

- **"R.I.C.E."** for monitoring response to bolus:  
  - **R**espiratory rate (falling = good; rising = overload)  
  - **I**nput/output (urine >0.5 ml/kg/hr)  
  - **C**apillary refill (<2 sec)  
  - **E**motional state (awake, alert)

**7 High-Yield Pearls:**
1. **Always check the fluid chart** – the most common error is inadequate re-assessment of volume status.
2. **Use Hartmann's as default** for most medical patients; only use 0.9% saline if hypercalcaemia or hypochloraemia is present.
3. **Bolus 500 ml, reassess** – never give a "2 L stat" without evaluating response.
4. **If lactate is >4 and not falling after 1 L, escalate** – may be occult sepsis or ongoing bleeding.
5. **The elderly patient on diuretics** may have both hypovolaemia and hyponatraemia – fix the volume with isotonic fluid first.
6. **Crystalloid + colloid?** In modern practice, avoid synthetic colloids (starch) due to renal harm. Albumin has niche roles (liver disease, burns).
7. **A "trick" question:** Patient with CHF and hypovolaemia – use small 250 ml boluses of Hartmann's with frequent reassessment; do not withhold fluid simply because of heart failure.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Giving 5% dextrose for hypovolaemia – it distributes into total body water (5% stays intravascular) → cell swelling, hyponatraemia
- Ignoring the giving set: use a blood-giving set for blood, and standard IV giving set for crystalloid; air must be removed from the line before connection
- Not labelling the bag and line with patient name, fluid type, and rate – medication error risk
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Parameter | When to escalate | Who to call | Action |
|-----------|------------------|-------------|--------|
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** | Any single score 3 (e.g., RR ≥25, SpO2 ≤91%) or aggregate 5 or more after initial fluid | Ward registrar or medical team | Reassess, consider sepsis, need for HDU/ICU |
| **Persistent hypotension** | SBP <90 mmHg after two 500 ml boluses (or MAP <65) | Senior registrar / ICU outreach | Consider vasopressors (noradrenaline), central line, echo |
| **Lactate rising or >4 mmol/L** | Not improving after 1 L fluid | ICU outreach | Urgent review, likely need for inotropes/perfusion support |
| **Urine output <0.3 ml/kg/hr for 2 hours** | Despite adequate fluid challenge | Renal team / ICU | Risk of acute tubular necrosis; consider renal replacement therapy |
| **GCS drop >2 points** | Any new confusion or fall in GCS | Crash team if airway concern; otherwise medical registrar | Reassess airway, check glucose, consider CT head (bleed?), check for electrolyte cause |
| **Chest auscultation: new crackles** | Fluid overload suspected | Stop fluid immediately; call registrar or cardiology | Diuretics (furosemide 40 mg IV), consider non-invasive ventilation, review fluid balance |

**Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥5 → immediate clinical review within 1 hour. Lactate >4 mmol/L despite initial fluids → immediate ICU referral. GCS ≤8 → protect airway, call anaesthetics.

rawMarkdown:
## Setting Up an IV Infusion
**One-liner:** Process of delivering intravenous fluids or medications directly into the bloodstream for resuscitation, maintenance, or drug administration – a core resuscitation and ward skill.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
A 72-year-old man is admitted from a care home with two days of diarrhoea and vomiting. He is drowsy, has sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, slow cap refill (~4 seconds), and a BP of 85/50 mmHg with a heart rate of 110 bpm. His urine output over the last 4 hours is negligible. Your FY1 colleague calls you to "set up an IV infusion" for him.

- **Drowsy, dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes** → severe dehydration (fluid loss >10% body weight)
- **Slow cap refill, tachycardia, hypotension** → hypovolaemic shock – immediate need for rapid volume expansion
- **Low urine output (<0.5 ml/kg/hr)** → hypoperfusion of kidneys; fluid resuscitation may prevent acute kidney injury
- **Diarrhoea and vomiting** → isotonic fluid loss (Na+ ~140 mmol/L lost), so mainstay is crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann's / 0.9% saline)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- The "silent hypovolaemia": elderly patient with isolated confusion, no tachycardia (beta-blocker) → check lying/standing BP, cap refill
- The "wet hypovolaemia": patient presents with oedema (e.g. liver disease) but is intravascularly depleted – need careful fluid challenge
- The "distributive" mimic: septic patient with vasodilation looks similar but needs different fluid strategy (balanced crystalloid + early antibiotics)

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
IV infusion is the delivery of a sterile fluid (crystalloid or colloid) into a vein via a cannula, giving set, and infusion pump or drip stand. It serves three main clinical purposes: **resuscitation** (rapid bolus to correct hypovolaemia), **maintenance** (replace daily losses when oral is impossible), and **replacement** (match ongoing losses like diarrhoea or NG output). It matters because it is the most common intervention in hospitalised patients, directly impacts haemodynamics, electrolyte balance, and outcomes. Common pitfalls: giving the wrong fluid (e.g. 0.9% saline in hyperchloraemic acidosis), too fast (fluid overload), too slow (inadequate resuscitation), or failing to monitor.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**Fluid compartments:** Total body water ~60% in men, ~50% in women. 2/3 is intracellular (ICF), 1/3 extracellular (ECF). ECF divides into interstitial (75%) and intravascular (25%). Losses from ECF (e.g. diarrhoea) shrink both interstitial and intravascular spaces – hence dry mucous membranes (interstitial) and low BP (intravascular).

**Starling forces:** Capillary hydrostatic pressure pushes fluid out; plasma oncotic pressure (mainly albumin) pulls fluid in. In hypovolaemia, hydrostatic pressure falls → reduced filtration → tissue dehydration. IV crystalloids distribute quickly across the ECF (only ~25% remains intravascular after 1 hour). Colloids (e.g. Gelofusine) stay longer intravascularly but carry risks (renal injury, cost).

**Why signs occur:**
- **Tachycardia** → baroreceptor reflex activated by ↓BP, sympathetic output increases HR to maintain cardiac output
- **Hypotension** → decreased stroke volume (↓preload) → reduced cardiac output → low BP
- **Slow cap refill** → vasoconstriction in skin (compensatory to preserve vital organ perfusion)
- **Oliguria** → renal hypoperfusion triggers renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system – conserves water and Na+

**Connecting to pattern recognition:** The vignette's vital signs signal a severe ECF deficit. Rapid infusion of a balanced crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann's) will acutely expand the intravascular compartment, lifting BP, heart rate improves, and urine output resumes – confirming the diagnosis.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
Indications for IV infusion fall into clinical scenarios – think of as "why does this patient need a drip?":

| Category | Common Cause | One-line "stem clue" |
|----------|--------------|----------------------|
| **Hypovolaemic** | Haemorrhage (trauma, GI bleed) | "Bright red per rectum" or "open fracture" |
| | Dehydration (vomiting/diarrhoea) | "Sunken eyes, reduced skin turgor" |
| | Burns (major) | "Blistering over 20% BSA" |
| **Distributive** | Sepsis | "Hot, tachycardic, hypotensive, history of infection" |
| | Anaphylaxis | "Sudden onset urticaria + stridor after drug" |
| | Pancreatitis | "Epigastric pain radiating to back, raised amylase" |
| **Maintenance** | Nil by mouth (pre-op, ileus) | "NBM since midnight" |
| | Impaired swallowing (CVA, frailty) | "Drooling, poor gag" |
| **Replacement** | High stoma output | "Dark green liquid in bag >1.5 L/day" |
| | NG losses | "2000 ml aspirate per shift" |

**Most common cause in general medical/surgical patients:** Dehydration from oral route failure (infection, post-op ileus, gastroenteritis).

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
You don't need a formal "diagnosis" to start IV fluids – it's clinical. But investigate to guide **type** and **monitoring**.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why order it | How to interpret |
|---------------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| **Urea & Electrolytes** | Urea, creatinine, Na, K, Cl, HCO3 | Assess renal function, electrolyte derangements, and type of dehydration (isotonic/hypertonic/hypotonic) | Raised urea:creatinine ratio (>20:1) suggests prerenal AKI (needs fluids). Low Na? Use isotonic fluids. High K? Avoid potassium-containing fluids until corrected. |
| **Full Blood Count** | Hb, HCT, WCC | Rule out haemorrhage (falling Hb), infection (raised WCC), or haemoconcentration | HCT >0.45 in a male with dehydration suggests hypovolaemia. Falling Hb + tachycardia = consider bleeding. |
| **Lactate** | Venous/arterial lactate | Marker of tissue hypoperfusion | Lactate >2 mmol/L suggests unmet oxygen demand – fluid resuscitation indicated. Normalising lactate = good. |
| **Urine output** | ml/kg/hr | Most sensitive monitor of end-organ perfusion | <0.5 ml/kg/hr for >2 hours → likely hypoperfusion. |
| **Blood gas (VBG/ABG)** | pH, pCO2, HCO3, lactate, base excess | Identify metabolic acidosis (e.g. lactate, HCO3 dropped) | Base deficit <-5 mmol/L = need for volume expansion. |
| **CVP** (central line) | Central venous pressure | Used in ICU to guide fluid responsiveness (not routine on ward) | CVP <8 mmHg suggests hypovolaemia – but dynamic measures (passive leg raise) are better. |

**Single finding that confirms urgent need for IV infusion:** A lactate >4 mmol/L with hypotension – start 500 ml bolus immediately, while ordering cultures and bloods.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

#### Immediate (first hour) — Resuscitation focus
- **Identify need:** Is this shock? (SBP <90, HR >100, lactate >2, altered mental state)
- **Gain access:** Large-bore peripheral cannula (18G or bigger) in antecubital fossa or forearm
- **Start with:** 500 ml bolus of **Hartmann's solution** (sodium 131, chloride 111, lactate 29 mmol/L) over 10-15 minutes (or via pressure bag if critical)
- **Why Hartmann's?** It is isotonic, balanced – less hyperchloraemic acidosis risk than 0.9% saline. For haemorrhage, give blood first (O negative) if available.
- **Reassess immediately:** After 500 ml, repeat pulse, BP, cap refill, lactate, urine output
- **If no improvement:** Give another 500 ml. If still hypotensive → call senior, consider other causes (sepsis, bleeding) and need for vasopressors / blood

#### Ward (24-72h) — Maintenance and replacement
Once shock corrected, move to maintenance or replacement:

- **Maintenance:** Use **Plasmalyte 148** or **Hartmann's** at 1 ml/kg/hr (e.g., 75-year-old 70 kg → 70 ml/hr). Monitor U&E daily.
- **Replacement:** Add on losses – e.g. diarrhoea 1 L/day → replace ml-for-ml with Hartmann's.
- **Potassium:** If K+ low, add KCl to the bag (e.g., 20 mmol KCl over 4 hours, peripheral line – concentration ≤40 mmol/L to avoid phlebitis).
- **Monitor:** Urine output >0.5 ml/kg/hr, daily weight (best indicator of fluid status), check for oedema, crackles on chest auscultation.
- **Stop early:** As soon as patient can eat and drink adequately – early feeding reduces infection risk.

#### Long-term — Tailored to underlying condition
- **Home IV therapy** (e.g., short bowel, stoma losses): Consider peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC) for long-term access. Use customised bags (macronutrients ± electrolytes). Monitor for line infections, thrombosis.
- **Renal impairment:** In established AKI, restrict fluids to insensible losses + urine output. Use hypertonic solutions with caution (risk of hypernatraemia).
- **Heart failure:** Fluid overload risk – give cautiously, consider furosemide if signs of failure.

#### Common pitfalls
- **DO NOT** give Hartmann's in the same line as blood (calcium chelates citrate → clot) – flush or use separate access.
- **DO NOT** use 0.9% saline for large volume resuscitation (>2 L) – causes hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis and may worsen AKI.
- **DO NOT** forget to reassess after each 500 ml bolus – fluid overload is as dangerous as under-resuscitation.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
IV infusion is a treatment, not a disease. But the clinical scenario of "patient needing IV fluids" can be mimicked by conditions that look like hypovolaemia but are not:

| Feature | Hypovolaemic Shock (need IV fluids) | Cardiogenic Shock (need inotropes/PCI) | Septic Shock (fluids + antibiotics) | Anaphylaxis (fluids + adrenaline) |
|---------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **History** | Vomiting, diarrhoea, blood loss | Chest pain, prior MI, dyspnoea | Fever, UTI, pneumonia | Drug/insect exposure, sudden onset |
| **Exam** | Dry mucous membranes, reduced turgor, cool peripheries+ | Raised JVP, lung crackles, cold/clammy | Warm peripheries initially, bounding pulse, fever | Urticaria, wheeze, stridor, swelling |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia | ST elevation / new LBBB / ischaemia | Sinus tachycardia ± non-specific changes | Sinus tachycardia |
| **Lactate** | Elevated (tissue hypoperfusion) | Elevated (hypoperfusion) | Elevated (aerobic glycolysis) | May be normal early |
| **Blood pressure** | Low, narrow pulse pressure | Low, often with raised diastolic | Low despite wide pulse pressure | Low (distributive) |
| **Response to 500 ml bolus** | BP improves, HR ↓, urine output up | No improvement or worsens (fluid overload) | May improve, but often needs vasopressors | Improves transiently; needs adrenaline |
| **Key differentiator** | Fluid losses | Raised JVP + crackles | Fever + history | Itch + stridor + exposure |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
Miss the need for IV fluids → irreversible shock → acute kidney injury (AKI), ischemic hepatitis, lactic acidosis, multi-organ failure, death. Conversely, giving fluid when not needed (e.g., cardiogenic shock) → pulmonary oedema, hypoxia, intubation.

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Fluid overload** | Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, leg oedema | Crackles on auscultation, raised JVP | Stop fluid, give furosemide 40 mg IV (if hypotensive – discuss with senior first) |
| **Phlebitis** | Pain, redness, swelling along vein | Local tenderness, palpable cord | Remove cannula, rest, warm compress, consider anticoagulation if extensive |
| **Infection** (septic thrombophlebitis) | Fever, rigors, swollen limb | Purulent discharge from cannula site, + blood cultures | Remove cannula, culture tip, start empirical antibiotics (flucloxacillin + gentamicin if severe) |
| **Air embolism** | Sudden chest pain, dyspnoea, confusion | Tachycardia, hypotension, "millwheel" murmur (rare) | Left lateral Trendelenburg position, 100% O2, call crash team |
| **Electrolyte derangement** | Weakness, arrhythmia, confusion (e.g. hyponatraemia, hypo-/hyperkalaemia) | Check daily U&E | Adjust fluid composition (e.g., add KCl if low, avoid 0.9% saline if hypernatraemic) |
| **Extravasation** (especially with irritant drugs) | Pain, swelling, blistering at site | Device not flushed well, palpable swelling around vein | Stop infusion, aspirate drug, elevate limb, apply cold/warm compresses, refer to tissue viability team |

**Late-presentation patterns (stems that test "what happens if untreated"):**
- Post-operative patient with low urine output and confusion: missed hypovolaemia → AKI → need for dialysis
- Elderly patient with pneumonia: given 2 L saline, develops pulmonary oedema → intubation

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **"S.A.L.T."** for choosing crystalloid type:  
  - **S**odium content (isotonic with plasma)  
  - **A**cid-base balance (avoid hyperchloraemia)  
  - **L**actate / buffer (Hartmann's has lactate, metabolised to bicarb)  
  - **T**onicity (only isotonic for peripheral infusion)

- **"R.I.C.E."** for monitoring response to bolus:  
  - **R**espiratory rate (falling = good; rising = overload)  
  - **I**nput/output (urine >0.5 ml/kg/hr)  
  - **C**apillary refill (<2 sec)  
  - **E**motional state (awake, alert)

**7 High-Yield Pearls:**
1. **Always check the fluid chart** – the most common error is inadequate re-assessment of volume status.
2. **Use Hartmann's as default** for most medical patients; only use 0.9% saline if hypercalcaemia or hypochloraemia is present.
3. **Bolus 500 ml, reassess** – never give a "2 L stat" without evaluating response.
4. **If lactate is >4 and not falling after 1 L, escalate** – may be occult sepsis or ongoing bleeding.
5. **The elderly patient on diuretics** may have both hypovolaemia and hyponatraemia – fix the volume with isotonic fluid first.
6. **Crystalloid + colloid?** In modern practice, avoid synthetic colloids (starch) due to renal harm. Albumin has niche roles (liver disease, burns).
7. **A "trick" question:** Patient with CHF and hypovolaemia – use small 250 ml boluses of Hartmann's with frequent reassessment; do not withhold fluid simply because of heart failure.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Giving 5% dextrose for hypovolaemia – it distributes into total body water (5% stays intravascular) → cell swelling, hyponatraemia
- Ignoring the giving set: use a blood-giving set for blood, and standard IV giving set for crystalloid; air must be removed from the line before connection
- Not labelling the bag and line with patient name, fluid type, and rate – medication error risk

### 10. Escalation Criteria
| Parameter | When to escalate | Who to call | Action |
|-----------|------------------|-------------|--------|
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** | Any single score 3 (e.g., RR ≥25, SpO2 ≤91%) or aggregate 5 or more after initial fluid | Ward registrar or medical team | Reassess, consider sepsis, need for HDU/ICU |
| **Persistent hypotension** | SBP <90 mmHg after two 500 ml boluses (or MAP <65) | Senior registrar / ICU outreach | Consider vasopressors (noradrenaline), central line, echo |
| **Lactate rising or >4 mmol/L** | Not improving after 1 L fluid | ICU outreach | Urgent review, likely need for inotropes/perfusion support |
| **Urine output <0.3 ml/kg/hr for 2 hours** | Despite adequate fluid challenge | Renal team / ICU | Risk of acute tubular necrosis; consider renal replacement therapy |
| **GCS drop >2 points** | Any new confusion or fall in GCS | Crash team if airway concern; otherwise medical registrar | Reassess airway, check glucose, consider CT head (bleed?), check for electrolyte cause |
| **Chest auscultation: new crackles** | Fluid overload suspected | Stop fluid immediately; call registrar or cardiology | Diuretics (furosemide 40 mg IV), consider non-invasive ventilation, review fluid balance |

**Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥5 → immediate clinical review within 1 hour. Lactate >4 mmol/L despite initial fluids → immediate ICU referral. GCS ≤8 → protect airway, call anaesthetics.
